data.title: Verizon: Caution Is Warranted, Despite The Strong Fundamentals (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In January this year, I issued a buy rating for Verizon's (VZ) stock, due to strong financial performance and an apparent undervaluation based on its dividends. Since then, VZ's price climbed significantly and by now reached $50 per share, which was my estimated fair value at that time, based on a dividend discount model. At this point, I no longer think that it is attractive to start a new position or to add to an existing one, and I believe a hold rating is more warranted. Despite reporting strong Q4 earnings, I believe that the increased debt YoY and the uncertain interest rate environment warrant caution.

Update on the business

I believe that Verizon's business remains attractive fundamentally, especially during times of significant market volatility. In the most recent quarter, the firm also impressed with its earnings and subscriber growth figures. Here are a couple of key figures that investors should keep in mind and monitor looking forward. Total postpaid phone net additions in Q4 reached 616,000, making it the strongest quarter of additions since 2019. Revenue grew in the wireless segment, both for equipment and for services. They also managed to add as many as 372,000 broadband customers. All this means that the demand for the firm's services remains strong.

Looking forward, Verizon has ambitious plans. First of all, significant cost reduction is foreseen after the Frontier acquisition. Operating cost synergies are expected to reach $1 billion by 2028, and an operational turnaround, which includes a workforce reduction, could lead to $5 billion in cost savings annually. These are significant numbers on the cost side, and I am excited to see how it will play out over the coming years. I do have a positive view on these ambitions, however.

It is not only the cost side of things that is appealing. Total retail postpaid phone net additions are expected to increase significantly, potentially reaching as much as 1 million. Mobility and broadband revenue growth is expected to be more modest, around 2% to 3%, while wireless service revenue is expected to stay flat. When looking at the free cash flow figures, VZ aims to reach $21.5 billion, which would represent a 70% growth compared to 2025.

In my view, Verizon's business and its ambitions remain attractive, and I do believe it is still worth owning the stock going forward.

Update on valuation

It is not only the fundamentals that I am happy with. The firm remained committed to returning value to its shareholders via dividends and share buybacks. In fact, VZ recently committed to buying back $25 billion worth of stock, which is a significant amount considering the company's $214 billion market cap. The firm aims to spend roughly $3 billion this year on the buybacks. They also managed to increase their dividends by 2.5%, bringing the quarterly payment up to $0.7075 per share, which is equivalent to an annual yield of 5.6%.

In January, I valued the firm's equity using a dividend discount model, with a required rate of return of 7.13% and a dividend growth assumption of 1.1% at $50 per share, which is very close to the current market price.

I believe that these assumptions are still valid, and the previously established fair value is still justifiable. This means that the upside potential from the current price levels is relatively limited. I do believe, however, that even at these price levels, Verizon remains an attractive holding for income-focused investors.

If I wanted to be a bit more optimistic, I could assume a near-term dividend growth rate of 2.5% for the next 5 years, which aligns with VZ's ambitions. This could bring the fair value to $54 per share, representing an upside potential of roughly 6% from the current price levels.

I believe this margin of safety is not high enough currently.

Why do I say that?

Since I last wrote about the firm, risks have increased for Verizon's business, primarily driven by the war in the Middle East. One may ask, what does Verizon have to do with the war and why would it be impacted at all?

With the ongoing war, energy prices skyrocketed, sending the Brent crude oil prices above $100 per barrel. This may not have a direct impact on VZ's operations and business, but it could have a significant inflationary effect if prices were to remain elevated.

To combat inflation, the Fed needs to manage the interest rates carefully. With inflationary fears ahead, they are not likely to cut the rates significantly this year, and expectations about a potential rate cut later this year worsened significantly. Now some even expect a rate hike to combat inflation.

This is a significant development for Verizon's business, as their capital structure contains significant amount of debt. In fact, their total unsecured debt increased by as much as 10%, reaching $131.1 billion. With higher debt, and potentially higher interest rates ahead, VZ's interest expenses could see an increase, which in turn could put a negative pressure on the firm's profitability.

Conclusions

All in all, I believe that Verizon's business remains attractive fundamentally. Significant cost savings and subscriber growth are forecasted for the coming years, which are anticipated to lead to a very positive impact on the free cash flow figures.

From a valuation point of view, I am becoming somewhat more cautious. I believe that the firm is approximately fairly valued now. Risks, however, since my last writing have significantly increased, mainly driven by the ongoing war in the Middle East. Elevated energy prices, inflation, and therefore elevated interest rates are likely to negatively impact Verizon's profitability, as their capital structure is highly reliant on debt.

For these reasons, I believe a hold rating is more justified right now.

data.title: IVE: Recent Outperformance Is Unsustainable
data.description:
The iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE), launched on 05/22/2000 and managed by BlackRock Fund Advisors, offers access to value stocks within the large-cap segment of the U.S. equity market. It charges an expense ratio of 0.18% and has $50.6 billion in AUM.

The case for value is now weaker because an opportunity cost seems more likely. The drivers that contributed to its superior performance relative to the broader market are not sustainable. The oil supply shock, mega-cap underperformance, and the software selloff have been key relative-performance tailwinds that are now headwinds for value-focused funds.

Methodology

This ETF tracks the S&P 500 Value Index, which evaluates every S&P 500 constituent based on value and growth factors to separate them depending on their value and growth scores. For value, it uses book value to price, earnings to price, and sales to price. For growth, it uses 3-year EPS change over price, 3-Year sales per share growth, and 12-month price momentum.

Each constituent is classified as either value, growth, or blended based on the ratio of Growth rank / Value rank. Those with the strongest value traits will represent 33% of the parent index market capitalization, while those with the strongest growth characteristics will represent another 33% of the parent index. The remaining 34% is classified as "blended" because value and growth traits are more balanced based on the ratio.

For the S&P 500 Value Index, all value and some blend stocks are selected. When it comes to the blend basket, some stocks are selected for inclusion based on how close they are to the midpoint of the value region versus that of the growth region. The closer a blend stock is to value, the more of its market cap will be considered for the weight it will have in the index. As such, IVE is not a pure-value fund and can realize a higher correlation to the S&P 500.

Last, the index weights the stocks based on their market caps, with a 10% cap per stock and a 22.5% cap for the group of stocks exceeding 4.5% each. Reconstitution is done annually after the close of the third Friday of December, and rebalancing occurs on a quarterly basis.

Portfolio

When looking at the top 10 holdings, it's worth keeping in mind that the top 3 names, Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), and Exxon (XOM), constitute 12.45% of the fund. Apple, as the top pick, implies meaningful exposure to a premium consumer hardware franchise with a strong services ecosystem. But the more relevant implication for the time being is that it provides mega-cap tech exposure that the market isn't rewarding anymore. Amazon has fallen out of favor due to its plans to spend even more on AI infrastructure in 2026 than it did last year. Exxon Mobil shares have surged recently due to the oil supply shock that the Iran war caused and, because of that, are vulnerable to a reversal if current de-escalation efforts succeed or the supply shortfall is offset (i.e., reserve releases, rerouted exports).

Cyclical sectors (Financials, Industrials, Consumer Cyclical, Energy, Basic Materials) account for almost half of the portfolio, which is consistent with the usual hunting grounds for value stocks. But because of the large-cap bias here, investors also get meaningful exposure to tech stocks with value traits. The tech allocation, along with communication, utilities, and real estate stock weights, implies a significant exposure to rate-sensitive equities at around 28%.

As of February 28, the 30-day SEC yield is 1.64%. This is not a dividend-focused ETF, so unless a significant price drawdown occurs, the yield is unlikely to become more meaningful anytime soon.

Performance

Relative to its benchmark, IVE has performed well, without major tracking differences. In the last fiscal year, it underperformed the index by only 19 bps; in the last 5 years, it underperformed by 20 bps; since inception, it underperformed by 17 bps.

However, it has underperformed the Vanguard Value Index Fund ETF (VTV), a cheaper alternative with an expense ratio of 0.03%, and its pure-value counterpart, the Invesco S&P 500 Pure Value (RPV).

More recently, it has outperformed its parent index, though, by a meaningful margin, being flat YTD while the index is down 3.4%.

A contributing factor has been IVE's Energy exposure, which is twice that of S&P 500 funds like the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). The Iran war has caused an oil supply shock that bid up the oil price. In just one month, it surged by more than 40%.

Another important factor has been that the market has fallen out of favor with mega-cap tech stocks. The Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) has been down 10.6% YTD, while the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), a proxy for broad market participation, is up 1.1%. IVE's top holdings makeup is very different from that of the S&P 500, which has significant exposure to the Mag7.

Another headwind for the S&P 500 has been the software stock selloff that occurred recently. With IVE having about half the tech exposure that S&P 500 ETFs do, it was more insulated from an increased risk of software disruption.

Risks

A relevant long-term risk for buy/hold investors is IVE's potential underperformance relative to VTV due to the significant difference between fees, as we saw above.

In the short run, you should keep in mind that the latest drivers of IVE's relative outperformance relative to the broader market are unreliable and imply a potential opportunity cost.

For one, the repricing of oil was quick and sharp. It has been in a consolidation phase for some time, and there are hints of the oil supply getting some relief. President Trump recently announced a 10-day attack pause on Iran's energy plants. While the Hormuz chokepoint hasn't normalized yet, another bearish shift for oil was Iran's goodwill gesture of allowing 10 oil tankers to pass through the Strait. All in all, the Iran war is the relative-performance tailwind that can become a headwind for IVE due to its Energy exposure.

Additionally, the mega-cap tech underperformance is mainly tied to higher hyperscaler AI-related CapEx and a broader risk-off sentiment. Stocks such as Amazon, Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOG) have been punished on news of higher investments in the AI infrastructure buildout, as investors seem to be demanding ROI evidence before they further bid up shares in such enterprises. Such a situation is unlikely to persist for long, as most of the hyperscalers agree that the demand for AI services is outpacing supply. When this becomes expressible through financial results, value funds with no meaningful exposure to such names can underperform.

Also, the software stock selloff is a thing of the past and unlikely to resume. The software disruption scare is connected to the risk that AI agents pose for software companies. However, the risk is overstated; the case for AI adoption is strong, but a significant across-the-board disruption is weaker as it depends on how the value proposition of software offerings is anchored to the platforms versus the databases. A recovery in the industry has already begun and will benefit the broader market more due to its high tech exposure.

Last, I must highlight again that the exposure to interest-rate-sensitive sectors is significant with IVE. As you may be aware, the Fed maintained its Fed Funds rate in the last FOMC meeting, and there is a heightened risk for rate hikes if inflation rises from here. Based on the 30-Day Fed Funds futures prices, the chance for a rate hike in the next meeting was 0% just a month ago, and now it has risen to 4.1%.

Conclusion

All in all, IVE has underperformed its peers in the long run, and though it performed well against the S&P 500 recently, that is unlikely to persist. I am rating it a hold.

For a cheaper way to get value exposure, VTV is the better fit. As for the heightened interest-rate risk I discussed, the pure-value alternative, RPV, deserves a mention as it has lower long-duration-like exposure at 13.7% (tech, real estate, communication, utilities).

Do you agree with this? Let's have a discussion below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: CHPY: Better Than Single-Stock ETFs (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the YieldMax Semiconductor Portfolio Option Income ETF (CHPY), I issued a hold rating due to the skepticism regarding its strategy and long-term potential. Since then, the fund has provided attractive total returns that outperformed the S&P 500. With the ongoing pullback in the technology market, you would expect CHPY to be performing poorly. However, the reality is that CHPY has held up quite well, so I wanted to revisit the fund's value proposition and outlook for 2026. After closer assessment of the fund and its utility, I am upgrading my rating to a buy.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CHPY's share price has increased by nearly 21.1%. The fund has displayed its ability to participate in the positive momentum of the semiconductor sector. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 78.52% over the same time frame. The main appeal of CHPY is its ability to provide a high dividend yield that now sits above 45%, which is paid out on a weekly basis. Due to the fund's unique portfolio strategy, this massive dividend yield hasn't caused the fund's share price to erode over time. However, there is still a risk of this happening at some point in the future if the semiconductor sector experiences prolonged headwinds.

Despite the optimism on CHPY, I believe that the fund has a very specific use case so it may not be the best choice for every investor. For instance, CHPY makes the most sense as an accompanying position for investors that already hold a diverse portfolio of growth equities. I would still consider CHPY to be a high risk holding, so it may not be a good fit for income investors seeking dividend reliability. Furthermore, the fund potentially exposes investors to the current challenges within the technology markets since the assets are concentrated.

Fund Strategy Is Superior To YieldMax Siblings

According to the latest fund overview, CHPY now has total net assets of $423.5M that are allocated across a diverse range of semiconductor assets. I've covered the majority of the YieldMax suite and the focus on synthetic options tends to lead to severe levels of NAV erosion over time, likely due to the inability to directly participate in the upside growth of its portfolio. This is where CHPY stands out from the rest of its YieldMax siblings. Instead of taking a single-stock approach like peer YieldMax funds, CHPY provides diversification across many different high quality companies. The benefits of this approach are as follows:

  1. CHPY isn't at the whim of a single earnings report.
  2. Semiconductors are volatile so the diverse holdings help smooth out drawdowns.
  3. Weightings can shift at any time based on market conditions.

Furthermore, CHPY actually holds the equities that it writes options against. Looking at the top holdings for the fund, we can see that Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding at 7.91% of assets. This is followed by many other high quality businesses, including Broadcom (AVGO), Lam Research (LRCX), ASML Holdings (ASML), and Marvell Technology (MRVL), just to name a few.

Since CHPY actually holds the stocks they write options against, the fund utilizes a standard covered call spread strategy. This means that management will utilize an OTM (out-of-the-money) option with a strike price that sits above the current market price of an asset. This allows for some upside growth to be captured alongside positive market growth. The option premiums collected from these calls are used to support the high distributions being paid out on a weekly basis.

In order to support weekly distributions, the fund typically writes options that expire within a ten-day window. In the early days of YieldMax funds, they typically relied entirely on synthetic long positions. So this shift to holding the actual equities has substantially improved performance. When comparing CHPY against some of the more popular single-stock peers out there, we can see the severe price difference over the last year.

Who This Fund Makes The Most Sense For

My personal investment style is to create a hybrid portfolio that provides both growth and income. With the fallen valuations across the technology sector, I have prioritized accumulating many growth positions. I believe that we must accumulate sectors when they are out of favor in order to achieve alpha. My portfolio now has many growth positions that require me to patiently wait until market conditions correct. So for the hybrid investor, CHPY connects the two approaches as a bridge. The fund's included option strategy means that the upside growth that can be captured is limited.

However, CHPY's ability to harness the volatility of the market and collect option premiums allows us to get paid without having to wait for the market to recover. Therefore, CHPY makes the most sense for an investor that has a bullish outlook on technology and semiconductors, but also believes that the road forward will be filled with uncertainty and volatility. Right now we have the unknowns related to the Strait of Hormuz, rising unemployment, and massive capex spending levels to build out AI infrastructure. Sentiment has also become an increasingly powerful force to market momentum, so we don't know how much longer these headwinds will remain relevant.

What I do know is that CHPY has been able to deliver resilience during a time of uncertainty and the distributions have successfully offset the recent declines. For instance, we can compare CHPY's total return against the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector (IGV), Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), and the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) over the last six months. We can see that CHPY has successfully offset these peer ETFs and kept up with the returns of SOXX over the same time frame.

Furthermore, the weekly payouts can be utilized by investors that want to have income to take advantage of suppressed valuations. The weekly distributions mean that investors will always have some sort of capital that can be reinvested back into other areas of their portfolio. For instance, I tend to manually reinvest my weekly distributions into more blue-chip steady dividend growth positions. This dividend wheel approach can help us rebalance our portfolio without needed to use our own invested capital.

Forward looking, the AI sector is becoming a lot more selective. Only a year ago, any mention of AI on an earnings call helped stimulate growth. The market conditions are different now as investors want to see companies move into their monetization phase. Since CHPY is actively managed, the fund is capable of repositioning itself to expose investors to the companies that are actually showing earnings growth as a direct result of AI.

My optimism with CHPY comes from the fact that the AI sector is now shifting to the monetization phase. The market is clearly punishing companies that aren't yet delivering earnings growth yet. This is where CHPY's active management comes into play because the fund can position itself with the semiconductor companies that are already securing revenue from the higher capex spend in the market. This is the benefit of an active management approach, as opposed to being locked into passively tracking an index. When it comes to the semiconductors, there are many clear examples where these companies are already profiting from the AI craze. Here are some examples that clearly demonstrate how CHPY is aligning itself with the best choices out there:

  • Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM): already experienced a 30% increase in revenue for January and February.
  • Marvell Technology: management expects revenue to approach $11B in FY27. Furthermore, the data center revenue increased by 46% year-over-year.
  • Broadcom: the revenue from semiconductor solutions increased by 52% year-over-year.

If my thesis is correct and these semiconductor companies are capable of growing this sort of growth, CHPY is will be able to partially participate in the upside growth within the parameters of its call spreads. Through very strong bull markets, the fund will also be able to participate in that upside growth when the stocks move past the upper threshold of its spread. So although there are still some limitations to the growth, CHPY is better positioned to capture upside growth against the YieldMax single-stock counterparts.

An even better scenario for CHPY would be if the indices continue to hover around its current range for the remainder of the year. If no progress is made on the Strait of Hormuz issue, CHPY may be able to efficiently harness the volatility of the markets and keep the distributions within a consistent range. For long-term investors, these large distributions can be a reward for the patience and it increases the potential for outperformance against the semiconductor's muted growth.

Risks

Despite the fund providing direct equity exposure to semiconductors, there are still many risks that investors should consider. The reality is that a large dividend yield of ~45% may be unsustainable over a longer time frame. For instance, volatility levels may decline and this can lead to lower option premiums received from its call spread strategy. Therefore, the fund may be faced with the scenario of occasionally paying out more than it actually earns from its premiums. This is where NAV erosion starts to occur.

The fund has been able to survive because the semiconductor sector has held up a bit better than the rest of the technology markets. Capex spending is already a sensitive topic for the hyperscalers so I imagine that any extension in spending could give investors fatigue. If companies don't see their revenues substantially improve over the next twelve months, this can lead to lower volumes for hardware orders, which would then negatively impact the semiconductor industry.

Although the fund directly holds equities, the upside growth is still sacrificed for a high current income. So if the sector were to face a significant drawdown, CHPY's ability to recover from this would be questionable. The fund will still be prioritizing a high rate of payouts, which steals from its overall growth potential.

Another potential risk would be a scenario where margins are squeezed for these businesses. There is already data to suggest that the cost of essential memory chips are already spiking. For many companies within CHPY's portfolio, this means that profit can shrink and slow the growth across the industry. For instance, high bandwidth memory costs five times more than standard memory and these contracts are typically secured for a long-term time frame. However, rising costs can threaten the this structure if prices are hiked to keep up with demand. So although the semiconductor market is estimated to reach a value of $720B by 2030, margins could shrink.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared weekly distribution of $0.4912 per share, the current dividend yield sits slightly above 45%. However, the yield will consistently change since new distributions are declared every single week. Since payouts are directly determined by the success of the underlying options strategy, the amounts can change over time. Looking at the history below, we can see that the highest payout was $0.6229 per share last year, while the lowest payout was $0.3488 per share. So based on market conditions, the level of income received can vary over time.

However, the historical data suggests that CHPY has done a solid job at keeping payouts within a tight range over the last year. While I wouldn't anticipate any sort of traditional dividend growth, I do believe that this sort of payout consistency should be commended. This is because it enables investors to create their own sort of dividend growth by implementing the wheel strategy that I previously mentioned. Consistent payouts means that investors can accumulate more shares of CHPY over time, which will lead to higher payouts. Conversely, the payouts also be manually reinvested into more traditional growth ETFs, such as the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

Since payouts can vary, I do not recommend CHPY to be utilized by retired investors seeking stability. CHPY is best utilized as a tool as part of a greater portfolio that can harness the volatility of the index. This has to do with the fact that CHPY isn't a simple buy-and-hold position. If the semiconductor industry were to experience a sharp decline, I would have no problem selling out of this to protect capital.

Furthermore, investors need to consider that the distributions received from CHPY may not offer tax-efficiency for investors. While it is possible for some payouts to be tax-efficient, the latest distribution is revealed to contain no return of capital. This means that the latest distribution was likely supported by net investment income, which has the highest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, a position in CHPY is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on CHPY to a buy due to the fund's portfolio structure. The fund provides direct equity exposure to the highest quality businesses in the semiconductor sector and can partially participate in the expansion of AI. The fund's covered call spread strategy helps generate premiums during this period of volatility, which has resulted in CHPY holding up a lot better than some of the most notable peers out there. However, CHPY isn't a straightforward buy-and-hold ETF. I think that CHPY makes the most sense as an accompanying position for investors that already have a diverse portfolio of assets. However, a downturn in the semiconductor sector could result in NAV erosion for CHPY over time.

data.title: Finally Using My Cash For Big Yields
data.description:
The layout has been adjusted to lead with the tickers and charts. Why? Because I think it allows the article to flow more naturally. Like it or hate it? Let me know in the comments.

The High Yielders

The charts compare the common shares from the following mortgage REITs and BDCs:

Agency mREITs

Hybrid mREITs

Originator / Servicer

Commercial

BDC

AGNC

EFC

RITM

FBRT

ARCC

NLY

MFA

PMT

BXMT

OCSL

DX

RC

GPMT

FSK

TWO

CIM

GAIN

ORC

MITT

MAIN

ARR

ADAM

GBDC

CHMI

SLRC

TSLX

CSWC

OBDC

TPVG

BXSL

The Charts

Mortgage REITs and BDCs:

Preferred shares and baby bonds:

Definitions for preferred shares:

  • FTF stands for "fixed-to-floating". It means the share is fixed-rate, but will begin floating based on SOFR. We may still refer to LIBOR, but LIBOR simply means SOFR + 26.161 basis points.
  • FTR stands for "fixed-to-reset". These shares are currently fixed-rate, but will eventually reset their dividend rate based on the 5-year treasury rate plus a given spread. They typically continue to reset every 5 years thereafter. At least in theory. That's pretty far away, but those are the terms.
  • FTL stands for "fixed-to-lawsuit". It only gets applied for PMT because they were the only mortgage REIT (that we know of) where management announced that "floating" really means a fixed dividend rate that never changes. PMT was sued over their actions. If you're not familiar with it, see this article on the lawsuit.
  • Floating stands for a share that is floating. Pretty obvious, right? This is the adult version of "FTF". The rate is typically updated every 3 months.

Commentary From The REIT Forum

It's been way too long since I posted this series. I apologize for the delay. As you may have heard, the market has been a bit volatile. I've been quite active in trading over the last several weeks as we build our positions. The higher interest rates are a substantial headwind for some of the securities we cover, but can even be favorable for some of the others. My favorite area for purchasing lately has been the floating-rate preferred shares and baby bonds. We cover many of them on The REIT Forum, as you could guess from the charts above.

Preferred Shares

The floating-rate preferred shares can be appealing because many are now trading at a nice discount to call value. With fewer projected cuts to the Fed Funds Rate, these preferred shares are looking at higher dividends. So, what pushes the price down? Well, credit spreads have been widening. As the market sees increasing risks, credit spreads go up. I think we have some nice opportunities in the sector now, so I've been building those positions.

When the market was less concerned about risk, we saw quite a few of these shares achieve prices high enough to make the yield-to-call negative. Now opportunities are opening up again.

We recently mentioned initiating a position in NLY-I (NLY.PR.I). Share values declined since then, but only slightly. As we predicted, there was healthy demand sitting around $25.00. Shares are down, but only to $24.88, because investors find the yield (about 9.15%) quite appealing, and preferred shares under $25.00 tend to gather more attention.

NLY-F (NLY.PR.F), which was frequently overvalued (severely negative yield to call), now has an 8.2% annualized yield to call.

While the lower-risk preferred shares like those from Annaly Capital Management are only down slightly, we've seen much larger declines in some of the higher-risk shares.

Following the dip in baby bonds, there is only one preferred share (in this sector) that still has a negative yield to call. That would be DX-C (DX.PR.C). Management is careful and has done a solid job with the portfolio. Yet the preferred shares carry a floating-rate dividend that includes a 5.461% spread. That's too large relative to the actual risk, so investors bid for the shares until they drive the yield-to-call negative.

Baby Bonds

The nice thing about the baby bonds is that the maturities are not all that far off. Consequently, even a material increase in Treasury yields doesn't move the price that much. If the bond matures in 3 years and Treasury rates rise by 33 basis points (such as 3.73% to 4.06%), it only merits a decline in the price of about 1%. That results in much lower volatility for the portfolio. So when I spot baby bonds going on sale with yields to maturity around 10%, that's pretty attractive. We're looking at big coupons combined with a modest gain for reaching maturity. I waited a few months for some of these opportunities to show up and they are finally here. So I'm happy to be able to build those positions.

Relative Value

To demonstrate the spread in relative values, I like using this image:

The only advantage for PMTU is the earlier maturity. It matures in late 2028, rather than early 2030 or mid-2030. In exchange for that, investors get more call protection, a vastly better yield to call, and pick up 120 basis points on yield to maturity.

There are two main scenarios for the next 2.5 years:

  • If PMTU gets called, then we are most likely dealing with lower interest rates and thinner credit spreads. In such a scenario, PMTV and PMTW would probably trade at a nice premium. That clearly favors PMTV and PMTW over PMTU.
  • If PMTU is left outstanding until maturity, then PMTV and PMTW will have collected an extra 110 to 120 basis points in yield to maturity over that time. But what if the prices of PMTV and PMTW went down? If PMTV and PMTU underperform over the next 2.5 years, their prices must be down by more than 1%. That would give them a pretty high yield to maturity for the final stretch. It's not impossible, but it is improbable.

This is a pretty common failing in the market. It has regularly overvalued PMTU relative to the other two baby bonds.

Since I'm mentioning these baby bonds, I need to mention that they have different ex-dividend dates. That's pretty strange. Most REITs design their baby bonds to have the same ex-dividend date. Consequently, in any analysis comparing the baby bonds, you also want to consider dividend accrual. Currently, PMTV has the most with $.35. PMTW is in second place with $.18. The difference of $.17 in dividend accrual enables PMTV to be more attractive even though the share price is higher by $.11.

Common Shares

If you're in the mood for the even higher-yielding common shares, the valuations are finally coming back down. Many of the mortgage REITs are down 15% to 18% from their 52-week highs. The BDCs are down much more though. I pulled a few of the columns from one of our sheets for subscribers to demonstrate:

This image really isn't doing the sheets justice. But the goal here was simply to communicate the information quickly. It did the job.

We saw quite a few BDCs setting fresh 52-week lows today. My BDC allocation is pretty small and it's doing alright overall. I didn't start purchasing until the sector was getting pounded. My biggest loss in the BDCs is down about 12.5%. Yes, it's down, but that was the worst-performing BDC purchase. Some of my positions in the sector are still up.

I think the BDC sector is much more attractive today than we've seen in a long time. We had weeks where we published with zero bullish ratings for the sector and sometimes very few neutral ratings. Those weeks we had mostly a wall of bearish ratings.

You know the worst part about a wall of bearish ratings? Everyone is looking for something to buy! They are rarely looking for someone to tell them to just chill with some cash and some baby bonds and wait for things to improve. Then, when valuations finally improve, many investors are no longer interested in the sector.

Market timing isn't my thing. I spend more time hunting for the individual shares that represent a better value than peers. I would rather be modeling out future cash flows than trying to predict when people will want to buy everything or dump their portfolio. Can I say modeling future cash flows is fun? Is that too nerdy?

If you're looking for bargains, this is how they show up. They arrive when everyone has a reason not to want them anymore.

Being Active

I picked up a few equity REIT positions today, but I was also active in the preferred shares and baby bond space. 16.19% of my portfolio right now is in baby bonds and preferred shares I purchased in March 2026. We had quite a bit of cash saved up going into the month. That doesn't count any capital I already had invested in those names. It is only the value of the shares in that sector that were purchased this month. I won't be able to add as many new positions over the next month as our cash allocation is dramatically smaller now.

What can we do when the cash position gets small? We can still look to optimize by trading between similar investments at materially different prices. We've done that for many years and it's a great way to enhance performance. Most investors are used to the idea of buying or selling stocks in a sector to look for an advantage. This is more attractive with baby bonds and preferred shares, because there is much greater certainty about future cash flows. It is never going to be perfect certainty, but we're looking at shares that typically trade in a pretty tight range. A tight range plus large cash flows leads to greater certainty about valuation. It's the fun of analyzing similar companies, but with lower volatility and big yields.

data.title: The Cost Of Uncertainty For SMBs
data.description:
Strong headline economic data masks a more difficult reality for small and mid-sized businesses (SMBs), where elevated costs and policy uncertainty continue to pressure margins and decision-making. Affordability has emerged as the central issue shaping both business conditions and the policy agenda.

At the same time, a fragmented, state-driven regulatory landscape is adding complexity and delaying investment. In this environment, SMB resilience will depend less on macro strength and more on the ability to stay agile amid persistent uncertainty.

Small and mid-sized businesses are contending with a widening gap between strong macroeconomic signals and a far more constrained operating reality. While GDP growth and employment data suggest resilience, business owners remain focused on persistent cost pressures, uneven demand, and an increasingly uncertain policy backdrop.

Affordability sits at the center of this tension. Elevated costs across labor, energy, insurance, and financing continue to weigh on margins, while tariff dynamics remain fluid and difficult to plan around. At the same time, policymakers are placing growing emphasis on affordability as a defining economic issue, one that is likely to shape both near-term policy decisions and the broader political landscape.

Compounding these pressures is a shift in where policy is being made. With limited progress at the federal level, states are taking a more active role, resulting in a fragmented regulatory environment. For SMBs, particularly those operating across multiple states, this creates added complexity, higher compliance costs, and greater uncertainty around future obligations.

In this environment, SMBs are increasingly prioritizing caution over expansion by slowing hiring, delaying investment, and preserving flexibility. Notably, as the backbone of U.S. employment, a sustained pullback in SMB hiring has the potential to dampen broader labor market momentum and, in turn, economic growth.

This environment reflects sustained uncertainty more than a typical cycle, and SMBs that stay agile and informed will be better positioned to manage near-term pressure and adjust as conditions improve.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Treasury Yields Snapshot: March 27, 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The yield on the 10-year note finished March 27, 2026, at 4.44%, the highest level since July 2025. Meanwhile, the 2-year note ended at 3.88%, and the 30-year yield ended at 4.98%.

The chart below overlays the daily performance of several Treasury bonds, starting from the pre-recession equity market peaks, along with the Federal Funds Rate (FFR) since 2007.

This next table shows the highs and lows of yields and the Federal Funds Rate since 2007.

A Long-Term Look at the 10-Year Treasury Yield

Here is a long-term view of the 10-year yield starting in 1965, well before the 1973 oil embargo that triggered the era of 'stagflation' (economic stagnation coupled with inflation)

Inverted Yield Curve

An inverted yield curve is when longer-term Treasury yields are lower than their shorter-term counterparts. The next chart displays the latest 10-2 spread. Typically, the spread turns negative for a period before rising again prior to recessions, as illustrated in the four recessions shown on this chart. For this reason, the 10-2 spread is widely considered a reliable leading indicator for recessions. The lead time between a negative spread and the onset of a recession varies, with recessions beginning anywhere from 18 to 92 weeks after the spread goes negative.

One false positive is seen in 1998, where the spread briefly went negative without leading to a recession. In the case of the 2009 recession, the spread went negative multiple times before rising again. Most recently, the spread was continuously negative from July 5, 2022, to August 26, 2024. The last time the spread was negative was on September 5, 2024.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date before a recession, the average lead time is 18.5 weeks, or about 4.25 months.

For another perspective on the yield curve, the 10-3mo spread below uses an even shorter-term maturity. The lead time to recessions based on this spread (after it turns negative) ranges from 34 to 69 weeks. Like the 10-2 spread, we see the same false positive in 1998, as well as multiple instances of the spread turning negative before rising again ahead of the 2009 recession. Recently, the spread was negative from October 25, 2022, to December 12, 2024. Since February 26th, the spread has swung back and forth from positive to negative territory.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date after the spread had been negative, the average lead time is 13 weeks, or about three months.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

The Federal Funds Rate influences the cost of borrowing for banks. When the Fed increases this rate, banks often raise their lending rates, which can impact mortgage rates, among other things. Therefore, a rising FFR generally leads to higher mortgage rates, and vice versa. However, this was not the case recently when the Fed began their rate-cutting cycle in September 2024 and mortgage rates moved in the opposite direction. With that said, mortgage rates have been on the decline as of late, following a similar fashion of the FFR. The latest Freddie Mac Weekly Primary Mortgage Market Survey put the 30-year fixed rate at 6.38%, its highest level since September.

Now let's see the 10-year against the S&P 500 with some notes on Federal Reserve intervention. Fed policy has been a major influence on market behavior.

For a long-term view of weekly Treasury yields, also focusing on the 10-year, see our latest Treasury Yields in Perspective update.

ETFs associated with Treasuries include: Vanguard 0-3 Month Treasury Bill ETF (VBIL), Vanguard Intermediate-Term Treasury ETF (VGIT), and Vanguard Long-Term Treasury ETF (VGLT)

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Let A Thousand Scenarios Bloom
data.description:
How bad could it get? That is the question on the minds of many investors as the war in the Middle East slogs on with no apparent clarity about, well, anything. Let’s do a quick check-in to see where we are as of this somewhat rainy Friday morning in the Washington, DC environs where we ply our trade.

When In Doubt, Equivocate

The S&P 500 stock index has lost around 7.2 percent of its value from its last record high, on January 27, to its close on Thursday. That is considerable, but the index has yet to cross either of Wall Street’s technical drawdown lines – a correction, which happens when the peak-trough reversal hits 10 percent, or a bear market, when the damage reaches 20 percent.

The relatively measured pace of this drawdown suggests that a multitude of scenarios are at play, with rosier projections imagining a near-term cessation of hostilities and eventual resumption of something approximating normal energy flows from the region to export markets, and less optimistic takes assuming that the absence of an obvious exit ramp today raises the probability of a structural quagmire.

What this implies is that, within the current picture of a mid-high single-digit percentage decline in stock prices, you have assumptions ranging from $200 oil to $60 oil by the time summer rolls around, with all the attendant secondary and tertiary effects in the broader economy.

About that Broader Economy

Let’s step back for a minute from the war and think about that broader economy as it exists today. We have finished tabulating corporate sales and earnings results from the fourth quarter of last year, and it was, in fact, a pretty good quarter.

S&P 500 earnings per share grew 13.4 percent over the quarter, a much better result than the 7.1 percent consensus projection economists were making as the fourth quarter got underway.

Earnings season for the first quarter of this year will kick off in a couple of weeks, and here too the outlook is fairly cheery, with the EPS growth consensus currently priced in at 13.0 percent – again, this is a higher projection than earlier estimates.

In other words, even with the near-certain prospect of higher inflation looming – the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development came out this week with an estimate of US inflation rising to 4.2 percent – the consensus among economists who follow these things is that companies will continue to enjoy double-digit profits growth.

Of course, just as the price trend in the S&P 500 reflects those myriad prognostications by the Pollyannas and the Cassandras of the market forecasting world, so too do assumptions about the health of corporate earnings depend in no small part on how things fare in the Gulf.

Here is one example: helium. You may have had no personal exposure to this element other than shoving a handful of helium-filled balloons into your car on the day of your three-year-old daughter’s birthday party.

But helium is a critical input for the advanced memory and training chips employed by artificial intelligence models. The vast majority of these chips are manufactured in either South Korea or Taiwan.

A large portion of the helium they need for the manufacturing process comes from – you guessed it – the Gulf region and has to pass through – you guessed it again — the Strait of Hormuz. Inventories are running low, and there have been some rumors circulating this week that Taiwan’s helium supplies were down to just a couple of weeks or so.

Given that spending on AI infrastructure was plausibly the largest single driver of US growth in 2025, it obviously should be of no small concern to contemplate the possibility of significant ruptures in the supply chain that feeds AI spend (it also serves as a useful reminder that, for all the talk of a rapidly deglobalizing world, these supply chains are still very much interconnected across multiple regions and nations).

This is just one example of what we mean by secondary or tertiary economic effects. A full suite of scenarios is currently at play. With time, though, the practical range of outcomes is going to winnow down, either to the upside or the downside. The decision makers responsible for whatever happens next do not have unlimited time on their side.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A List Of Worries That Risk Flipping Much Worse
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

The realisation has dawned that there is no quick 'off switch' to this war, and that's being reflected in the rates discount

President Trump’s recent efforts to calm things were nothing more than a flop, as Iran countered with their own requirements and conditions for an end to hostilities. The Strait of Hormuz is the real battle of contention right now, as it’s a pain point that Iran knows it can hurt the US with.

As we commence week five of the conflict, we’re in a standoff mode between the two sides, and, meanwhile, the lobbing of munitions from both sides continues. The US has options, ranging from 'the benign' – pulling back and declaring victory, to 'the malignant' – placing troops on the ground to help force open the Strait. There are many other options in between, of course, but the game of interpretation takes place between these two extremes.

We’ve had a re-elevation in inflation breakevens, alongside a re-ratchet higher in the price of oil. Front-end breakevens are discounting US inflation approaching 4% in the coming quarters (vs. 2.5% CPI inflation now).

In consequence, Fed interest rate cut expectations have evaporated, and if anything, the market now attaches a greater probability to a hike than a cut as the next move. The structure of the Treasury curve is still pricing cuts, given that the 5yr area continues to trade rich versus the 2yr and 10yr interpolation.

But that richness is significantly less than it was. And market rates have been on the rise. The 10yr yield got close to 4.5% before easing lower a tad. That's quite a move from sub-4% just before the war broke.

The equity market has morphed to a clearer risk-off phase. The only silver lining is that credit spreads today are at or below their 5yr averages. But another interpretation is we’re on a journey wider if things don’t calm in a material fashion in the coming weeks.

The yield curve has squeezed flatter and higher, and the prognosis ahead is for even higher yields, at least until this thing sees a material positive change in sentiment. We're left with a whole series of arrows pointing resolutely in a troubling direction.

A dashboard of pain coming from the Rates Market

Things to watch – barometers of things beginning to boil out of control (USD basis and swap spreads)

And then there is a quirky basis premium being attached to the US dollar (on cross-currency swaps). This was practically zero before the war broke out. It’s now just below 10bp, albeit still not particularly high. In previous crises, it’s been a barometer of stress, and has been multiple times the current level.

Various patches to the capacity to get hands on US dollars over the years have dampened basis re-widenings during periods of stress. We’re not anticipating a spiral wider, but it's absolutely one to watch.

The other one to watch is the swap spread, and here we like to look at the 10yr tenor. It’s now just short of 50bp; so, the 10yr Treasury yield is 50bp above the 10yr 'risk-free' SOFR rate.

Basically, it’s the price the US Treasury needs to pay over and above the risk-free rate as a cost for fiscal untidiness. Should Treasuries come under specific selling pressure from a credit perspective, it will show up here. The danger level would be 60bp, or even the approach of it.

The market price action in rates is broadly centred on a pricing in of more and more risks coming from a longer war

Adding some flavour, we’ve seen three Treasury auctions come and go in the past week, and they were all awful. It’s indicative of a market that requires a discount to get business done. There has been some selling of fixed income, and in particular high yield.

But it has not been dramatic, as there is still demand out there. The selling itself has not been the driver of market movements, as it’s been relatively tame. Rather, the market is steadily re-pricing the aforementioned risks that have driven the bulk of the price action.

The market environment craves a positive delta. Even a clear sign that things are getting less worse would be a good starting point. As it is, we are slavish to the whims of key decision makers. There is a huge relief trade to be had here, on a path-switch to a quicker resolution in the war. But until then, the risk remains for a large lurch in a bad direction.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Adidas: A Buy At Undemanding Valuations As Inventory Set To Normalize
data.description:
Adidas (ADDYY) (ADDDF) has been able to deliver a strong recovery to its top line over the past year, but the quality of that growth remains a point of debate, which has definitely weighed on ADR's lackluster performance over the same period.

The "brand of the three stripes" has shown a dynamic where inventory is increasing faster than sales growth, and its cash flow has been highly influenced by shifts in working capital. The main question is whether the turnaround has been driven by real demand or through balance sheet expansion.

While it is difficult to state precisely that Adidas trades in deep-value territory, I believe that at least it is possible to argue that the market has priced ADDYY as if the recovery will fall short. The market's skepticism of the management team's comments that inventory growth will normalize and that Adidas can sustain high-single-digit top-line growth for the next three years is unexciting. However, I believe that, especially trading in the current undemanding multiples, Adidas finds itself in a position that does not need to outperform to re-rate, only to execute.

Therefore, I'm leaning constructive on Adidas' thesis today, seeing some asymmetry as the trend of margin normalization progresses toward the company's guidance.

Inventory, Cash Flow, and the Quality of Growth

The first thing I look at in Adidas' thesis is whether the growth thesis is today being financed in a healthy way or "forced" via inventory and working capital.

When we look at the last ten quarters reported by Adidas, we can see strong revenue growth in the double digits, especially from September 2024 until now (with the exception of September 2025), but at the same time, extremely volatile operating profit in the same period. That alone tells a story that growth has not been "clean" or consistent, and the margin still seems to be far from stabilizing.

As a kind of "rule of thumb", ideally, inventory levels should not grow more than sales for a long time but at least in line with revenue, or rather, below it (which indicates efficiency). The chart below helps to illustrate well how Adidas' correlation between revenue and inventory growth has been since 2Q23.

In 2023, Adidas was going through a classic problem of overstocking (strongly negative inventory, ~-20%, and weak revenues), clearing collections (Yeezy), putting pressure on margins, and hindering healthy growth.

Normalization came in 2024, with inventories going to positive levels of growth, reaching sort of the "sweet spot" as the stock seemed to be rebuilding with demand (the best moment of the cycle, and no wonder recovery on ADDYY trading performance).

But in 2025, a yellow flag was raised again as inventory accelerated again more than revenue (~30% vs. ~10-15%), signaling an overbuilding scenario again, but which can be interpreted as (1) anticipating demand that may not come or (2) replenishing channels and betting on strong growth ahead.

In addition, the other chart below (which crosses cash from operations, changes in inventory, and net income) shows that Adidas' cash flow generation has been much more volatile than its net income, which also reinforces the relevant impact of working capital. It can be noted that throughout 2023 and parts of 2024, changes in working capital dominated the behavior of cash flows, with periods in which the release (or consumption of inventory) significantly distorted the operating flow without a direct correspondence with the performance of net income.

Perhaps the most evident point of this distortion was in 4Q24, when operating cash flow showed a sharp drop at the same time that profit did not collapse in the same way—i.e., indications that the problem was much more linked to working capital management than operations per se. FY25 was marked by a relevant recovery in operating cash flows, even though inventory variations continued to consume cash, but also suggested that other components of working capital are sustaining this recovery.

Interpreting the Inventory Rebuild

My initial reading of the correlation between revenue growth vs. inventory is that Adidas is potentially entering a risk zone again.

However, the management team highlighted in the last earnings call that this inventory growth is not a problem but a preparation for growth.

CFO Harm Ohlmeyer made it clear that Adidas "has been too disciplined on inventory in '23... we went too low" and is now preparing for further top-line growth in '26, based on three clear reasons listed: (1) World Cup build-up; (2) supply chain timing (earlier inbounds at year-end); (3) "clean" inventory with only 7% not being from the current season.

The most reassuring statement is along the lines that this disconnect between revenues and inventory will not be maintained for long:

“So rest assured that for the quarters to come, we will bring that [inventory levels] further down. And you should assume that we make progress in the first half of '26. And you can take me serious at year-end '26, you will see a number that is below the EUR 5.8 billion.”

For FY26, management is driving revenues by guiding high single digits and operating profits by ~€2.3 billion. It's not bad growth, but it's a slowdown from the 13% YoY sales growth reported in FY25.

Incorporated in USD, this growth is expected by consensus to be around ~5%, which is about 3% lower than what was guided before 4Q25. Worse than that is the more extended guidance until 2028 at the same levels of top-line growth and an EBIT margin expansion still being a "promise", showing a slower turnaround perspective.

At the same time, it is possible that Adidas is being too conservative in its guidance. But if demand fails to materialize—as current 2026 expectations suggest—the thesis of risk of inventory growth above sales growth returns to discount and margin pressure.

So I would say that, to a large extent, the underperformance of Adidas' ADS is in line with the fact that the market has not fully bought into the company's turnaround story.

Valuation, Risks, and What Comes Next

It seems very clear to me that Adidas is being priced as a still problematic business and far from something normalized. The company trades at a forward P/E of 13.3x, and a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.8x—all well below its main peers, Nike (NKE) (~33x P/E; ~21.9x fwd EV/EBITDA), On Holdings (ONON) (~19.8x P/E; ~11.8x fwd EV/EBITDA), and Deckers (DECK) (~13.6 P/E; ~8.8x fwd EV/EBITDA).

But according to Adidas' own guidance, with EBIT expected to go from ~€2.1B to ~€2.3B and margins heading towards ~10+, the prognosis is for strong profit expansion in the coming years—perhaps slightly below the previously expected pace.

If we put a visibility for P/E FY2 and FY3 of 9x and 6.8x, respectively, these multiples are very low if Adidas were to deliver its guidance at all. That being said, if Adidas merely manages to stabilize margins, deliver top-line growth in high-single digits while at the same time normalizing inventory, I see a plausible path for Adidas to go to a multiple of ~18-22x (peer range).

So much so that if we take into account the consensus that long-term EPS tends to grow at a CAGR of 14.15% over the next three to five years, Adidas today trades at a PEG of 0.94, indicating undervaluation.

This suggests that Adidas is in a position where the bar is so low that even outperforming is not necessary for a re-rating, just a normalization scenario—and I think that's plausible.

Of course, execution is key, and there are also exogenous factors (macro and geopolitical) in addition to tariff impacts and FX challenges that may delay normalization towards the proposed guidance.

However, I believe that, as much as Adidas' thesis today is under great skepticism, the undemanding valuations for a business that seems to be reorganizing itself for a normalization over the next 1.5/2 years (in terms of margin growth) appear to offer an attractive entry point.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
